items.q: What is the difference between quality control and quality assurance?
items.correct: Quality control inspects and tests outputs against tolerances; quality assurance is the whole system of procedures designed to prevent defects occurring in the first place.
items.misconception: QC and QA are the same thing — checking products at the end of the line.
items.random_wrong: QA stands for 'quick assembly' of standard parts.
items.why: Students merge the two terms; examiners expect QC as checking outputs and QA as the broader preventative system embedded throughout production.

items.q: Should designers always specify the tightest possible tolerance?
items.correct: No — tighter tolerances require more precise machinery and inspection, raising cost; good practice specifies the loosest tolerance that still guarantees function.
items.misconception: Tighter tolerances always mean a better, more professional design.
items.random_wrong: Tolerance describes how well a material resists heat.
items.why: Students equate precision with quality, missing the cost-precision trade-off that is central to design for manufacture decisions.

items.q: What does the critical path in a project plan show?
items.correct: The longest chain of dependent tasks, which fixes the minimum project duration; any delay on this path delays the entire project.
items.misconception: The critical path lists the tasks that are most dangerous and need safety checks.
items.random_wrong: The critical path is the conveyor route through the factory.
items.why: The word 'critical' suggests danger to students; in project management it means time-critical — tasks with zero float.

items.q: What does a Gantt chart display?
items.misconception: The forces acting on a structure
items.random_wrong: The chemical composition of a material
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Project tasks plotted against time. “The forces acting on a structure” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: What is the critical path of a project?
items.correct: The longest sequence of dependent tasks, setting the minimum project duration
items.misconception: The list of the most hazardous tasks
items.random_wrong: The route materials take through the factory
items.why: The answer examiners want is: The longest sequence of dependent tasks, setting the minimum project duration. “The list of the most hazardous tasks” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Which change best illustrates design for manufacture?
items.correct: Reducing the number of separate components in a product
items.misconception: Adding decorative features to impress customers
items.random_wrong: Specifying the tightest tolerance on every dimension
items.why: It's Reducing the number of separate components in a product. “Adding decorative features to impress customers” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: What is a go/no-go gauge used for?
items.correct: Quickly checking that a dimension lies within tolerance
items.misconception: Measuring the exact hardness of a material
items.random_wrong: Timing tasks on the critical path
items.why: Correct answer: Quickly checking that a dimension lies within tolerance. “Measuring the exact hardness of a material” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: A dimension is specified as 50 mm with a tolerance of plus or minus 0.5 mm. What is the maximum acceptable size?
items.why: Exact answer: 50.5 mm. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
